interpretations.interpretation: At the outbreak of World War I, multiple belligerent sacred political orders achieved maximum mobilizational strength by invoking the nation as a sacred collective body that dissolved all internal distinctions. Kaiser Wilhelm's dissolution of party divisions and Poincaré's 'sacred union' both demonstrate the mechanism: the sacred political order expands to absorb all individuals into a single sacrificial body at the moment of perceived existential threat.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Passages are well-attested verified texts. The mechanism is well-supported for the mobilization moment. Confidence capped given multi-nation unit of analysis.

interpretations.mechanismSummary: At war outbreak, national sacred political orders in Germany and France suppressed all internal distinctions and sacralized the nation as a collective body demanding total sacrifice — Kaiser Wilhelm recognized 'only Germans,' Poincaré invoked 'sacred union.'

claims.claim: The outbreak of World War I was accompanied by multi-national sacred political orders invoking sacred national unity that dissolved class, party, and individual distinctions — framing the nation as a sacred collective body for whom total sacrifice was both possible and obligatory.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Kaiser Wilhelm's 'I recognize only Germans' and Poincaré's 'sacred union' are well-attested public-domain texts verified via GHDI and firstworldwar.com. Both document the sacrificial-nationalist moment at war outbreak across two major belligerents. Confidence capped because the case covers multiple nations with variable outcomes; the claim holds for the mobilization moment but not uniformly across the war's duration.

passages.locator: Kaiser Wilhelm II, Address to the Reichstag, 4 August 1914; English translation in German History in Documents and Images (GHDI): https://ghdi.ghi-dc.org/sub_document.cfm?document_id=815
passages.text: I no longer recognize any parties; I recognize only Germans.

source.notes: Public domain. English translation verified at GHDI.

passages.locator: Raymond Poincaré, Message to the French Chambers, 4 August 1914; English translation at https://www.firstworldwar.com/source/poincare_aug1914.htm
passages.text: She will be heroically defended by all her sons; nothing will break their sacred union before the enemy; today they are joined together as brothers in a common indignation against the aggressor, and in a common patriotic faith.

source.notes: Public domain. English translation verified at firstworldwar.com and BYU WWI archive.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Maximum sacred-political-order-strength score is supported by both the German and French mobilization declarations dissolving internal distinctions and invoking sacred national unity. Confidence capped at moderate given the multi-nation unit-of-analysis and variable national trajectories after mobilization.

scores.reviewNote: Score value 5/5 defensible: collective-immortality-to-sacrifice mechanism (nationalist sacrifice discourse across all major belligerents) supports maximum sacred-political-order-strength. Confidence 0.60 reflects chain-requires-expansion and the multi-nation unit-of-analysis problem (the case aggregates several national orders). Hold at score-review; specify the unit of analysis before human-reviewed.
